posts.text: Ahh, so it's THIS vessel. This is ARISTA (9348493). She is Comoros-flagged (as far as being "foreign" goes) but is part of Iran's Shamkhani network. ARISTA has been under US OFAC sanctions since last summer. She's actually been stuck in this same spot since mid-March 2026; at https://t.co/tqqKnos5mt
posts.summary: A sanctioned Iranian tanker has been immobilized in the same location since mid-March 2026, potentially removing a vessel from Iran's oil export network and reducing available tanker capacity for crude shipments. The vessel's prolonged detention could constrain Iran's ability to transport oil to market while limiting global tanker supply.

posts.text:
🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE.

Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may be inflating exports a bit (from inventories). But whatever that contribution is, clearly June oil exports have surged to >95% of
posts.summary: The UAE has increased its oil exports to pre-war levels or higher in June, suggesting a significant recovery in Persian Gulf oil production capacity, though some of this surge may be attributable to the release of stored inventory.

posts.text:
COLUMN: The hottest commodity? 

One that's suffering from a major supply shock. It may sound like I’m talking about oil, but this is even more severe. Output is down 40% y-on-y and prices are up 80%.

The commodity in question? The anchovy. 

@Opinion https://t.co/tfgV2Ib8Vq
posts.summary: Javier Blas highlights that anchovy supply disruptions are experiencing more severe market dynamics than oil, with output down 40% year-over-year and prices up 80%. The comparison suggests anchovy shortages are creating a more acute commodity shock than current oil market conditions.

posts.text: It'll be interesting to learn how a vessel with an AIS-reported draft depth of 10 meters ran aground in 35-50 meters of water. https://t.co/eAlKhO0I82
posts.summary: Tanker Trackers highlights a discrepancy in AIS data for a grounded vessel, noting that a ship reporting a 10-meter draft ran aground in waters 35-50 meters deep, raising questions about data accuracy in maritime tracking.

posts.text:
Hormuz traffic holds steady

The Strait of Hormuz remained open and active on 30 June, with 34 verified crossings recorded and traffic evenly split by direction. The dataset showed a broad mix of commercial, energy-linked and support movements, while route visibility remained https://t.co/cVZHRte4Hy
posts.summary: The Strait of Hormuz maintained normal traffic flow on June 30 with 34 verified crossings and balanced directional movement, indicating continued stability in a critical chokepoint for global energy shipments. The steady commercial and energy-linked activity suggests no disruptions to oil transit through the strategic waterway.

posts.text:

✅Ending ruinous subsidies that drove up electricity prices
✅Reinstating federal land sales to get more oil and gas drilling

posts.summary: The Trump administration is pursuing increased domestic oil and gas production through federal land sales and offshore drilling expansion, while reducing energy subsidies, with stated intent to lower electricity prices for consumers.

Companies are rapidly changing their minds that artificial intelligence can “do it all” by rehiring employees to propel their businesses forward, as investors fret over the longevity of the ongoing AI
posts.summary: Based solely on the text provided, there are no oil market implications mentioned or discussed. The post addresses employer staffing decisions related to AI adoption and has no connection to energy markets.

posts.text:
1.9 MMbpd is about 73% of prewar production level (~2.6 MMbpd) in Kuwait already.

Up from barely more than 20% (<600 kbpd) on average in May

At the beginning of June, the national oil company said it would take 6-8 weeks to get back to 70%, so this is well ahead of earlier plan https://t.co/6qrNWrvDc7
posts.summary: Kuwait's oil production has rebounded to 1.9 million barrels per day, representing 73% of pre-war levels and significantly exceeding the national oil company's earlier timeline of 6-8 weeks to reach 70% capacity. This faster-than-expected recovery from May's production lows could help ease global oil supply concerns.

posts.text: US military deploys over 900 personnel to Venezuela for earthquake response https://t.co/wDhtIT08a6

# Oil Market Summary

The deployment of over 900 US military personnel to Venezuela for earthquake response could temporarily disrupt oil production if infrastructure damage occurs, though the immediate oil market impact depends on the earthquake's severity and geographic location relative to Venezuela's production facilities.

posts.text:
Citadel Hires Ex-Shell Power Trading Head to Lead Physical Push

Ken Griffin’s Citadel has hired Shell Plc’s former head of European power trading to lead a push into physical commodity markets in the region.

Rupen Tanna will join in September and be based in London to lead the
posts.summary: Citadel is expanding into physical commodity markets in Europe by hiring Shell's former head of European power trading, Rupen Tanna, to lead the effort from London starting in September. The move signals the hedge fund's growing interest in the physical energy trading sector.

posts.text: GLOBAL DATA CENTRES consumed almost 780 billion kilowatt-hours of electricity in 2025 up from 410 billion in 2020. Consumption has increased by an average of almost 14% per year. As a result, data centre consumption was equivalent to about 2.5% of global generation up from 1.5% https://t.co/PMQthn9IHe
posts.summary: Data centre electricity consumption nearly doubled from 2020 to 2025, growing at 14% annually, which could increase demand for natural gas and oil-based power generation to support this expanding energy need. The sector's rising share of global electricity generation—from 1.5% to 2.5%—highlights growing competition for energy resources that may put upward pressure on fossil fuel markets.

posts.text:
Russia's fuel shortages are rippling across Central Asia. Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Uzbekistan are seeing higher fuel prices, while Kazakhstan weighs requests to supply Russia even as it protects its own market. #Russia #Energy #Oil #CentralAsia 

https://t.co/po2PYpWYGS
posts.summary: Russia's fuel shortages are driving up energy prices across Central Asian markets, while Kazakhstan faces competing pressures to supply Russia and maintain domestic fuel availability. The regional supply disruptions highlight broader energy market instability stemming from Russia's domestic fuel constraints.

posts.text: A new analysis challenges the conventional view that tighter oil supplies always mean higher prices. The argument: recession, demand destruction, and government restrictions could push crude below $40/bbl while disrupting global supply chains. #OilMarkets #Energy #Recession
posts.summary: A new analysis suggests that crude oil prices could fall below $40 per barrel despite supply tightness, driven by recession-induced demand destruction and government restrictions that could also disrupt global supply chains. This challenges the traditional assumption that constrained oil supplies automatically lead to higher prices.

posts.summary: Canada has joined the IEA's Ministerial Advisory Panel on Electricity Affordability, reflecting the country's commitment to maintaining affordable energy prices as part of broader economic and energy security objectives.

posts.text: The last week of June can wreak havoc on the best analysis of US gasoline consumption. Last five year shave seen a variation of 1.058-million b/d in implied demand. I'm expecting a demand surge of 400,000 b/d or more from last week's 8.775-million b/d measurement.
posts.summary: Tom Kloza notes that late June gasoline demand data can be highly volatile, with variations exceeding 1 million barrels per day in recent years, and he anticipates a significant surge of at least 400,000 barrels per day from the previous week's measurement of 8.775 million barrels per day. This seasonal volatility makes it challenging to accurately assess US gasoline consumption trends during this period.

posts.text:
Lots of attention on refining 3-2-1 crack spreads vs. recent oil price movements (for good reason).

However, there are other things behind refined product tightness this go around.

RVO compliance costs, included in wholesale RBOB (gasoline blendstock) and ULSD is above $15/bbl https://t.co/D09wRVkjY4 https://t.co/sJp8Diyj9N
posts.summary: Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) compliance costs have added over $15 per barrel to wholesale gasoline and diesel prices, contributing to refined product tightness beyond what traditional crack spread analysis captures. This cost component is an important factor to consider alongside crude oil price movements when analyzing refining economics.

posts.text:
🗣️ "I don't know what we would do without you [@fbirol] & the @IEA in these turbulent times. You really play an invaluable role in giving us the data, helping us translate it & giving us advice”

posts.text:
Weekly arbs update: Europe remains long prompt, with North Sea barrels under pressure and Atlantic Basin flows seeing limited pull. That leaves DFLs carrying more of the adjustment.

What to watch: SoH transits could further disruption slow westbound Gulf flows and put a floor https://t.co/EIMbSXLQ9v
posts.summary: Europe has excess prompt crude supply with weak North Sea pricing, forcing Distillate Fuel Oil (DFL) markets to absorb additional supply adjustments while Atlantic Basin flows remain sluggish. Strait of Hormuz transits could further constrain westbound Gulf crude flows, potentially supporting prices.

posts.text:
Two large convoys of commercial ships were observed crossing the Strait of Hormuz, one of the clearest examples yet of what appears to be an increasingly common navigational tactic to keep maritime traffic safe.

Six freighters were seen entering the Persian Gulf closely together
posts.summary: Commercial shipping convoys are increasingly crossing the Strait of Hormuz together as a safety measure, with multiple freighters traveling in close formation through the Persian Gulf. This tactical shift reflects growing concerns about maritime security in a critical chokepoint for global oil transport.

posts.text:
Carbon capture faces political opposition, project delays and high costs, but demand isn't disappearing.

For hard to abate industries, CCUS remains one of the few viable decarbonisation options. @WoodMackenzie expects strong long term growth despite today's challenges.

Read
posts.summary: Carbon capture and utilization/storage (CCUS) faces near-term headwinds from political opposition, delays, and costs, but Wood Mackenzie expects long-term growth as it remains a critical decarbonization tool for hard-to-abate industries. The technology's continued development could support oil and gas sector decarbonization efforts despite current implementation challenges.

posts.text: Venezuela's oil exports fell slightly to 1.2 million barrels per day last month from 1.24 million bpd in May as two deadly earthquakes caused minor delays at terminals operated by state-run energy company PDVSA. https://t.co/uUMmEz4pLG
posts.summary: Venezuela's oil exports declined to 1.2 million barrels per day in June from 1.24 million bpd in May, with earthquake-related disruptions at PDVSA terminals contributing to the minor decrease. The modest drop reflects operational challenges that temporarily affected the country's crude production capacity.

posts.text:
U.S. Oil Recovery Threatened by Lower Prices

U.S. oil production accelerated to a record rate after the war with Iran and closure of the Strait of Hormuz boosted prices and dispelled some of the gloom hanging over the industry at the end of 2025.

Total production of crude and https://t.co/YnbgrLhSCN
posts.summary: U.S. oil production reached record levels following geopolitical tensions with Iran and the Strait of Hormuz closure, which elevated prices and improved industry sentiment after a challenging 2025. However, lower prices now pose a threat to sustaining this recovery in domestic oil output.

posts.text:
If upstream teams are flooded with data, how do they see what matters?

The industry shift isn’t about more data sitting in more dashboards. It’s about data that moves with your decisions, at the pace of your decisions. 

CERA Titan is about seeing the signals, not the noise, in https://t.co/o4LgyQzQ6I
posts.summary: S&P Global Energy highlights that the energy industry needs data systems designed for real-time decision-making rather than static dashboards overwhelmed with information. The implication is that upstream oil and gas teams require more responsive, actionable intelligence to effectively navigate market conditions and operational decisions.

posts.text: Refiner Phillips 66, which earlier this year agreed to a contract to buy directly from PDVSA, received at least two cargoes bound for the US, while trading firm Novum Energy is loading a cargo for July. At the Orinoco Belt, the Petromonagas project restarted a delayed coker.
posts.summary: Phillips 66 and other traders are receiving Venezuelan crude shipments despite sanctions pressure, while production at the Petromonagas project has resumed, signaling continued oil supply flows to the US market. These developments suggest Venezuelan crude exports remain active through direct contracts and third-party trading arrangements.

posts.text: Oil prices plunge 10% for the week as Hormuz traffic resumes; Brent settles at $71.99 - https://t.co/Eq9VxR42RR #GoogleAlerts
posts.summary: Oil prices declined 10% over the week as shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz resumed, with Brent crude settling at $71.99 per barrel. The resumption of Hormuz traffic appears to have eased supply concerns that had supported higher price levels.

posts.text: Oil Futures Pick Up After Dip To Pre-War Levels - Barron's - https://t.co/cPyANlgcrE #GoogleAlerts
posts.summary: Oil futures rebounded after declining to pre-war price levels, according to a Barron's report shared by Phil Flynn. The movement suggests a recovery in oil prices from their recent dip.

posts.text:
Clean product inventories are now at their lowest level since 2015. Global onshore crude stocks continue drawing nearly 4 mb/d.

Yet refinery runs can't keep pace. With forward margins well above seasonal norms, even if refineries run at maximum throughput it will take until https://t.co/U2dnMXzOry
posts.summary: Global crude oil inventories have fallen to their lowest levels since 2015 while refineries cannot process oil quickly enough to meet demand, suggesting tight market conditions and elevated refining margins may persist for an extended period.
